subject: ap-chemistry
title: Atomic Structure & Periodic Trends
stimulus: The complete photoelectron spectrum of an element in its ground state is represented below.

parts.prompt:
Based on the spectrum,
(i) write the ground-state electron configuration of the element, and
(ii) identify the element.
parts.scoring_notes: The photoelectron spectrum shows peaks corresponding to subshells. Calcium (Ca, Z = 20) has the configuration 1s$^2$2s$^2$2p$^6$3s$^2$3p$^6$4s$^2$ = [Ar]4s$^2$. The spectrum shows 5 peaks with relative intensities 2:2:6:2:6:2 consistent with Ca. The highest-energy ionization peaks correspond to the most tightly bound (innermost) electrons; the lowest-energy peak corresponds to the valence 4s electrons.

scoring_points.description: For the correct ground-state electron configuration of calcium: 1 point
wrong_examples: Writing [Ar]3d$^2$ — this is a common error; for Ca (Period 4, Group 2), the electrons fill 4s before 3d.
Writing [Ar]3d$^1$4s$^1$ — this is an excited state, not the ground state.
common_traps: Ca fills the 4s orbital before the 3d subshell. Ground state of Ca: [Ar]4s$^2$. The 3d subshell is not occupied in Ca.

scoring_points.description: For identifying the element as calcium (Ca): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: The element is Calcium (Ca). The PES shows 6 peaks consistent with 6 occupied subshells in Ca (1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s), with a total of 20 electrons.

wrong_examples: Saying potassium (K) — K has 19 electrons [Ar]4s$^1$; the spectrum shows 2 electrons in the valence shell (4s$^2$) → Ca.
Saying Ti or Sc — these fill 3d first after Ar; Ca fills 4s.
common_traps: Sum the electrons from all peaks: 2 (1s) + 2 (2s) + 6 (2p) + 2 (3s) + 6 (3p) + 2 (4s) = 20 → Z = 20 → Ca.

parts.prompt:
Calculate the wavelength, in meters, of electromagnetic radiation needed to remove an electron from the
valence shell of an atom of the element.
parts.scoring_notes: The ionization energy of the valence (4s) electrons of Ca is read from the PES as 0.980 × 10$^{-18}$ J per atom (given in the spectrum). Use E = hc/λ: λ = hc/E = (6.626 × 10$^{-34}$ J·s)(3.00 × 10$^8$ m/s) / (0.980 × 10$^{-18}$ J) = 1.99 × 10$^{-25}$ / 9.80 × 10$^{-19}$ = 2.03 × 10$^{-7}$ m.

scoring_points.description: For the correct setup E = hc/λ with the valence ionization energy from the spectrum: 1 point

required_elements: Valence ionization energy read from the lowest binding energy peak in the PES.
alternatives.correct_example:
Ionization energy (valence 4s electrons) = 0.980 × 10$^{-18}$ J/atom (from PES).
$\lambda = hc/E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.00 \times 10^8)}{0.980 \times 10^{-18}} = \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{9.80 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.03 \times 10^{-7}\text{ m}$

wrong_examples: Using the innermost (highest binding energy) peak — the question asks about the valence shell (lowest binding energy peak).
Using ionization energy in kJ/mol without converting to J/atom — divide by 6.022 × 10$^{23}$ to get per atom.
common_traps: Valence electron = lowest binding energy (leftmost/smallest peak in PES). E = hc/λ: use per-atom ionization energy in Joules. If given in MJ/mol, convert: divide by 6.022 × 10$^{23}$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct wavelength ≈ 2.03 × 10$^{-7}$ m: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: $\lambda = 2.03 \times 10^{-7}\text{ m}$ (in the ultraviolet region of the electromagnetic spectrum)

wrong_examples: Getting λ in nm but not converting to m — answer must be in meters.
Inverting the equation (E = λ/hc) — correct form is λ = hc/E.
common_traps: Rearrange E = hf = hc/λ: λ = hc/E. Answer in meters. UV region: ~10$^{-7}$ m = ~200 nm.
